intro:
Calculating wallpaper needs correctly prevents the frustration of running short mid-project — a serious problem since wallpaper lots can vary in shade between print runs. Our wallpaper calculator estimates the number of rolls needed based on room dimensions, wall height, and your roll's coverage area.

A standard US wallpaper roll covers approximately 56 square feet (but only about 42 sq ft after trimming and waste). European rolls are typically larger at 57 sq ft usable. Wallpaper with a pattern repeat requires extra rolls — a 24-inch repeat can waste one full roll per three applied strips.

This calculator uses your entered roll coverage directly for accuracy. Always buy 1–2 extra rolls from the same lot as a buffer, and verify the lot number on every roll matches before purchase.
howItWorks: Enter room length and width in feet, wall height (default 8 ft), and your roll's usable coverage in square feet (default 56 sq ft — adjust based on packaging). The calculator computes the four-wall area, divides by roll coverage, and rounds up to the nearest full roll.
interpretationGuide: Rolls needed is the minimum to cover all four walls. Always buy 1–2 more rolls from the same dye lot for pattern matching and repairs. If your wallpaper has a large pattern repeat, increase your order by 10–15% or calculate more carefully based on pattern repeat length.

faqs.question: How many rolls of wallpaper do I need for a 12×14 room?
faqs.answer: Wall area: 2 × (12+14) × 8 = 416 sq ft. At 56 sq ft per roll (standard single roll): 416 ÷ 56 = 7.4, so 8 rolls minimum. Allow 1–2 extra for waste: order 9–10 rolls.

faqs.question: What is a single vs. double roll of wallpaper?
faqs.answer: Wallpaper is sold in single and double rolls (bolts). Despite being twice as long, a double roll typically contains 56 sq ft — the equivalent of what used to be two single rolls (28 sq ft each). Modern 'single rolls' are usually the 56 sq ft size. Always check the usable square footage on the label, not just 'single' or 'double'.

faqs.question: How much extra wallpaper do I need for pattern matching?
faqs.answer: Wallpapers with small patterns (under 6 inch repeat): add 10%. Medium patterns (6–12 inch repeat): add 15–20%. Large patterns (12–24 inch repeat): add 20–25%. Random match (no repeat): 10%. Pattern matching wastes the most material at wall edges and joints between strips.

faqs.question: Should I subtract doors and windows from wall area?
faqs.answer: For conservative estimates, you can subtract about 20 sq ft per door and 15 sq ft per average window. However, many wallpaper installers advise against this — the savings are small and you risk running short. Most professionals recommend calculating the full wall area and using the saved material for future repairs.

faqs.question: What is a wallpaper dye lot?
faqs.answer: Like paint, wallpaper is printed in batches. Slight color variations exist between different production lots. Every roll used in the same room should have the same lot/run number. Buy all rolls for one room in a single purchase to guarantee the same lot.

faqs.question: Can I wallpaper over existing wallpaper?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended. Old wallpaper can bubble, peel, or transfer texture to the new layer. Additionally, moisture from new wallpaper paste can loosen the existing layer. Stripping and preparing the wall properly ensures a lasting, professional result.

faqs.question: How do I prepare walls for wallpapering?
faqs.answer: Fill holes and cracks, sand smooth, and apply a primer or wall size (a diluted adhesive that seals the surface and improves adhesion). New drywall should always be primed and allowed to cure before wallpapering. Properly prepared walls are essential for longevity.

faqs.question: What is peel-and-stick wallpaper?
faqs.answer: Peel-and-stick (self-adhesive) wallpaper requires no paste and is repositionable, making it popular for renters and DIY installers. It typically covers 28–30 sq ft per roll. It works best on smooth, clean walls and is less permanent than traditional wallpaper.

faqs.question: What tools do I need to hang wallpaper?
faqs.answer: Seam roller, wallpaper brush or squeegee, plumb bob or level (for straight vertical lines), measuring tape, sharp utility knife and cutting guide, tray for paste (traditional wallpaper), sponge for excess paste, stepladder, and seam tape for repairs.

faqs.question: How long does wallpaper typically last?
faqs.answer: Quality traditional wallpaper lasts 10–20+ years with proper installation and care. Vinyl-coated wallpaper is the most durable and cleanable. Paper-only wallpaper is less durable. Proper surface preparation is the biggest factor in longevity — well-prepped walls with quality adhesive can hold wallpaper for decades.
